item.title: Rabuka confirms elections will proceed as planned
item.description: Navitalai Naivalurua · 18/07/2026 Fiji will go to the polls whether or not a new or amended Constitution is in place.
item.content:encoded:
Navitalai Naivalurua · 18/07/2026

Fiji will go to the polls whether or not a new or amended Constitution is in place.

That is the message from Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka when asked by the media whether the next General Election will be held under a new or amended Constitution.

Rabuka says the timing of any constitutional amendment will take its own course, but the country will proceed with the election.

He adds that regardless of whether any changes to the electoral provisions are in place, Fiji will still hold the General Election.

Meanwhile, the Constitution Review Commission is now reviewing and analysing the submission received. The Commission must submit its final report and a draft bill to the President by August 31th, 2026.

The campaign period for the 2026 General Elections is now open.

The General Election must be held between 7th August, 2026 and 6th February, 2027.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Thousands of Taveuni students to be affected as bus company plans to stop services
item.description: Concerned parent Mikaele Legai of Vuna, Taveuni, says he received a letter from the head teacher of the school attended by his children advising parents of the planned suspension. Legai expressed concern about the impact the disruption could have on students, particularly those preparing to sit their Literacy and Numeracy Assessment next week.
item.content:encoded:
Concerned parent Mikaele Legai of Vuna, Taveuni, says he received a letter from the head teacher of the school attended by his children advising parents of the planned suspension.

Legai expressed concern about the impact the disruption could have on students, particularly those preparing to sit their Literacy and Numeracy Assessment next week.

He says the journey to school by bus normally takes between 40 minutes and one hour, but without bus services, students could be forced to walk for two to three hours each way.

According to Legai, the bus company has informed parents and guardians that rising transport costs, similar to those experienced in Suva, have prompted its request for an increase in student bus fares.

The Minister of Education Aseri Radrodro is looking into the matter

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Vodafone Fiji to build new mobile tower in Qila as it works to improve Taveuni coverage
item.description: The company says they are closely monitoring network performance to identify areas where demand has increased. They are also assessing upgrades to improve network capacity and service.
item.content:encoded:
The company says they are closely monitoring network performance to identify areas where demand has increased.

They are also assessing upgrades to improve network capacity and service.

Vodafone Fiji says it is aware that there are still parts of Taveuni where no mobile service is available from any provider.

They confirm they are building a new mobile tower in Qila as part of this year's network expansion plans. It says other areas of Taveuni will also be considered as the rollout programme continues.

Vodafone Fiji says the Government's Universal Service Obligation initiative is also helping telecommunications providers expand coverage to remote areas, including Taveuni.

The company is encouraging customers experiencing network issues to contact its Customer Care line on 123 so their concerns can be recorded and addressed as quickly as possible.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: COVID setbacks could not stop FNU researcher from winning national award
item.description: She was named 1st Runner-Up in the Research Excellence Award for Early Career Researcher . Dr Chand, who is from the College of Agriculture, Fisheries and Forestry, says she was surprised to receive the award, admitting she did not expect her name to be called.
item.content:encoded:
She was named 1st Runner-Up in the Research Excellence Award for Early Career Researcher .

Dr Chand, who is from the College of Agriculture, Fisheries and Forestry, says she was surprised to receive the award, admitting she did not expect her name to be called.

Her research examined how farmers receive information and how technology can improve communication with them, particularly during cyclones and other climate-related events.

She says the study looked at the types of media farmers prefer, the challenges they face in accessing information, and how Government policies can better support the use of information and communication technology in agriculture.

Dr Chand says completing the research was not easy.

She began her PhD during the COVID-19 pandemic while on scholarship in China but was unable to travel because of border restrictions. She completed her coursework online before travelling to China for her research proposal defence. She later returned to Fiji to collect data from farmers before going back to China to analyse the data and complete her thesis.

Although the research took about three years of active work, the overall journey lasted four years because of the disruptions caused by the pandemic.

Reflecting on her achievement, Dr Chand says she was proud to see many women recognised at the awards, saying it encourages more women to pursue research and innovation that can benefit Fiji.

Dr Chand says studying for a PhD can be mentally challenging and urged researchers to look after their mental health, spend time with supportive family and friends, and never be afraid to ask for help.

Dr Chand credits her husband, family, colleagues and the Dean of her College for supporting and encouraging her throughout her journey , saying their belief in her helped her reach the finish line.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Record 24 teams set to compete in 10th Bara Battle
item.description: The 10th edition of the Frank Hilton OrganisationBara Battle is set to kick off next Saturday, with a record 24 teams registered to compete in this year's tournament, the highest number of entries in the competition's history. This was shared with fijivillage News by the Chief Executive Officer, Frank Hilton Sureni Perera as the teams took part in the captains run this morning.
item.content:encoded:
The 10th edition of the Frank Hilton OrganisationBara Battle is set to kick off next Saturday, with a record 24 teams registered to compete in this year's tournament, the highest number of entries in the competition's history.

This was shared with fijivillage News by the Chief Executive Officer, Frank Hilton Sureni Perera as the teams took part in the captains run this morning.

Perera says preparations are progressing well ahead of the tournament.

Perera says teams held a brief captain's run today while organisers finalised programmes and other logistical arrangements for next week's event.

She says she is looking forward to a successful tournament and is encouraging members of the public to come out and support the Bara Battle.

The Bara Battle will be held at Albert Park on Saturday.

The organisation aims to raise funds for the Hilton Home, which requires $300,000 annually to operate, including wages for qualified caregivers providing 24/7 care, equipment, food, therapy, and other expenses associated with running a residential care facility.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj

item.title: Rabuka supports RFMF call to remove constitutional immunity clause
item.description: Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says he supports the Republic of Fiji Military Forces' call to remove the immunity provisions from the 2013 Constitution. S peaking to the media after the opening of the Salvation Army Alcohol and Drugs Recovery Centre, Rabuka says he is not protected by the immunity clause because he is free anyway.
item.content:encoded:
Prime Minister Sitiveni Rabuka says he supports the Republic of Fiji Military Forces' call to remove the immunity provisions from the 2013 Constitution.

S peaking to the media after the opening of the Salvation Army Alcohol and Drugs Recovery Centre, Rabuka says he is not protected by the immunity clause because he is free anyway.

Insert: PM on not protected – 17th July 2026

In its submission to the Constitution Review Commission, the Republic of Fiji Military Forces recommended that the constitutional immunity provisions be removed.

RFMF Commander Major General Ro Jone Kalouniwai says the systemic and institutionalised pattern of political instability that has fractured the nation since 1987 was not merely witnessed by the military but, at times, was built and maintained by its own hands.

He says the RFMF does not make that admission lightly, nor without genuine remorse for the role the institution has played in protecting a system that has fractured the moral soul of the nation.

Send your NEWS TIPS and VIDEOS to us on our Facebook or Instagram OR you can email us on tips@fijivillage.com.fj
